text: These days, most leaders I talk to are feeling the pressure to do something with AI fast. But if you're responsible for bringing AI to your organization, you might be thinking, where do I even start? In this video, I'm going to share a clear roadmap that leaders can use to drive AI transformation in their organizations. And if you're new here, welcome. I'm Shah. I'm a data scientist turned AI educator, and I've taught hundreds of leaders and builders from companies like Google, Microsoft, Deloitte, and many more through my live AI training programs. When I talk to leaders at mid-size companies, the story usually goes something like this. They are the senior leader that is responsible for driving AI innovation or AI transformation within their organization. Maybe they're the head of innovation, so they're used to stuff like this, or they've been given a dedicated title of AI transformation lead. But many times, this might just be an and responsibility of a VP of engineering, a chief strategy officer, a chief technology officer, the head of analytics, a chief product officer, and on and on and on. And so one pattern is there isn't a clear function that always picks up this responsibility. So leaders are coming from all different types of backgrounds and placed in this role to drive this transformation. But that's not the only challenge. From multiple sources, they're hearing the same thing. We need to do something with AI. This might be investors, whether that's shareholders or private investors, or if it's a private equity firm. This might be board members who are putting the pressure on the CEO or on the senior leadership directly. Could be the CEO passing along this pressure from the board or investors. Could be competition. You see some competitor rolling out an AI feature, or you hear that they are doing AI transformation within their organization, and your organization hasn't really gotten started on that. Or it might be your customers. I was talking to one senior leader, and he was saying it's actually their clients who are demanding that when they submit proposals, there's some kind of AI plan or AI workflow baked into it to keep costs down. So, there's definitely a lot of pressure to do something with AI, and then the senior leaders I talked to are just caught in the middle of all this. However, just doing something with AI isn't always a great idea. That's because without a clear goal, this is some of the things I'm seeing happen at companies. We have people focusing too much on using tools instead of solving problems. We have people that are shining shiny objects and AI trends as opposed to trying to generate value with the technology. And with a generic use AI mandate, teams are kind of left on their own trying to learn everything about AI and end up getting overloaded by all the information that's out there. You also will get teams that are learning things independently from different sources, and they end up rowing in five different directions, and then overall, the team doesn't really go anywhere. Ultimately, when there isn't a clear goal, there becomes this de facto goal of let's just use AI instead of let's transform the business. And the result of all this is teams spend a lot of time, money, and attention pursuing AI without making a whole lot of progress. But there is a better way. Instead of focusing on using AI, the focus should be on building AI-native systems. And the key point here is that transformation doesn't just mean adding a new tool to the way that you already do business. What it means is redesigning your business systems to fully maximize what you can get from this new technology. Just to show what I mean by this, the old way of doing business was humans would use applications to get their work done. So, here we have two team members. They have a whole set of applications that they use to get their work done. One team member is responsible for marketing analytics, the other team member is responsible for customer acquisition, and they work together so that the data analyst can support the GTM person so that they can get as many customers as possible. However, the new way that businesses are operating is humans plus applications plus AI agents. We still have the same people, we still have the same tools, but we're also adding these agents into the mix. And ultimately, teams will use these agents to get their work done faster, better, or just do more of it. Maybe the analyst uses a specialized agent to take Jira tickets and turn them into analyses in Excel. Maybe the GTM person will have a specialized agent that connects with Power BI, so that if she wants some ad hoc request, she doesn't have to bother the analyst about it. She can just go to this agent and do these very quick experiments. Finally, maybe there's an agent that assists the GTM person to ensure her CRM is always up to date and she is filling up her calendar with as many sales calls as possible. Just to give you an example of this, let's see how a B2B sales outreach process might change between the old way and the new way. So, with the old way, the GTM person might search for leads using an app like Apollo. Then, she'll export those leads into an Excel spreadsheet. Then, she will manually qualify the leads by searching for them one at a time on LinkedIn or Google. Once she's qualified a subset of the leads, then she'll load it into her CRM, which might be HubSpot. Then, she will add each of those leads to an automated outreach campaign, and then she'll reply to her responses via email and update the CRM accordingly. However, with a specialized GTM agent in the mix, it might look something like this. So, instead of manually searching for leads on Apollo, she might just kick off the prospecting workflow with a prompt to Claude. And Claude will have a specialized skill for searching across Apollo for the right customer avatar, and then qualifying that lead using specialized sub-agents that will go off and do deep research on every prospect. And then the result of this is Claude will generate a short list of qualified leads to reach out to. Then the GTM person can review and refine this lead list and then give the green light. Once she gives the green light, Claude can then use a different skill to add the leads to the CRM and assign them specific outreach sequences. Claude can draft replies to all of the responses, and then the human can review the drafts that Claude writes and then send them off. A few key things have happened when transitioning this process from the old way to the new way. First and foremost, the steps changed. This step of exporting leads to a spreadsheet is no longer needed cuz the GTM person isn't manually qualifying these leads themselves, so the workflow itself changed. The second thing is that the technology changed. There's no longer a need for Excel, there's no longer a need to use LinkedIn, there's no longer need to use Google search because that's all going to be baked into this agent. So this agent has a web search tool, and it will have just a different process for keeping track of the leads that it's trying to qualify. But the third and probably most impactful change is that roles and job descriptions change. So the GTM person went from doing this whole process end-to-end to just kicking off the prospecting with a prompt to providing oversight to the lead list and giving the green light. Also giving oversight to any of the emails that are being sent to prospects. So essentially most of this workflow is now going to be done by Claude. So, what may have taken 10 hours of work will just come down to 1 hour of work by this GTM person. And what this enables is instead of the GTM person being so focused in the weeds and on the execution of the workflow, the GTM person can now start working on the workflow instead of in the workflow. In other words, they can spend the time to analyze funnel metrics to see how many of our emails are actually being opened, how many people are actually replying, for the people who reply, how many are saying yes, how many are saying no. Of the people who say yes, how many people are actually showing up to sales calls. Of the people who show up to sales calls, how many people are actually closing. So, the GTM person can look at these high-level pipeline metrics and do targeted optimizations to improve the funnel. So, this new way of getting customers is not only going to be faster for the GTM person, it's not only going to result in reaching out to more people, but ultimately it's going to result in higher quality work because the human can focus on the higher leverage work in this workflow. So far, I've really just laid out the vision and the destination of what AI transformation might look like. However, most teams aren't at that point where their sales people can start creating custom agents and redesigning their workflows. A helpful roadmap here for getting teams from the old way of running their business to the new way is the following four-step roadmap. The first step is to equip team members with AI tools. The second is to educate them on how to use those tools effectively. The third is that they actually adopt the technology and use it in their day-to-day work. And then finally comes redesigning the business systems and ultimately transforming the business. Starting with step one, this is all about giving the right tools to the right people. Talking to one senior leader and they were talking about how they gave their engineers access to lovable so they could start building things. However, the problem with that is that engineers don't necessarily want to use lovable. They probably want to use Claude code, they want to use cursor, they want to use antigravity and so on. It's not only about acquiring the tools but acquiring the right tools for the right people. For non-coders, this might mean Microsoft 365 Copilot so they've got a whole suite of AI tools from chat to agents and more. Getting enterprise license of ChatGPT, getting enterprise license for Claude or Claude Co-work, or giving people access to Gemini. This technology choice will depend on what technologies you're already using. So if you're already a Microsoft shop, a lot of companies just end up going with M365 Copilot. If they're already a Google shop, they'll end up going with Gemini in their whole stack. But other teams will opt in for going with Claude or ChatGPT even if they have these existing technologies. And for coders, this is going to come down to the ecosystem of AI coding tools that are out there. Here are many popular ones. We've got Claude Code, we've got Cursor, we've got Codex from OpenAI, we've got Antigravity from Google, and we've got GitHub Copilot from Microsoft. I personally use Claude Code. I feel like it's the best tool right now for AI coding. But really any of these tools will be better than humans writing code from scratch. The second step is to educate. It's not just enough to give people tools because this technology is very new and people may not know how to use it most effectively. Educating is really on aligning teams on a shared understanding of AI and its best practices. The two main things that are worth learning here are core concepts. Things like what is AI even? What are large language models? How do they work? What are some best practices for writing good prompts? What is MCP and how do I set it up? And what are skills and how do I set those up? This will give team members a conceptual foundation for what this technology actually is. And then the second thing worth learning is tool-specific training. So once you have an idea of what this technology is and what it can do, then people need to learn, okay, how do I actually do this with my available tools? So this might be specific training for Claude or Claude code or Microsoft 365 Copilot or whatever it is. And giving people frameworks and step-by-step guides on how to customize and utilize these tools for their work. And the second big question here is how to learn. And as a lifelong learner, I give a lot of thought to this. And the way I think about it is that there are four key ways to learn. The first is by concept. So this will be giving people access to self-paced courses or having a trainer come in or having someone internally give a workshop or something like that or people self-learning on YouTube or whatever it is. This is just people learning the concepts, learning ideas. The second way to learn is learning by example. So it's not just enough to learn what the concepts are. People need to see those concepts applied to solving problems so they can start imagining what that might look like in their work. For example, anytime I lead a workshop or make a YouTube video, it's almost always going to be 50/50 split between laying down the conceptual foundation and then going through a concrete example. However, just learning the concepts and seeing someone else walk through an example is not enough to solidify their understanding. At some point, you need to get your hands on the keyboard and start using the technology and applying it to your own context. This is learning by doing. So getting team members to actually start applying this technology to their work. And then the final way to learn is to learn by teaching. Any opportunity for one team member to teach another team member is going to help everyone solidify their understanding. Here is where AI champions is actually a very powerful approach. What an AI champion is is someone that looks like me that is a little further ahead when it comes to AI teaching me something about it. This could be for like sales people. You have one sales person who's really killing it and building automations and using Claude or ChatGPT for their work. You have them lead a session on how they automated their prospecting process. And this can actually become kind of a fun thing where you have maybe weekly luncheon learns and the team members who are really doing cool stuff, they can tell everyone, "Hey, this is something I did a couple weeks ago." And not only do the attendees of the luncheon learn learn something and get some ideas on how they can apply the technology, but the person delivering the content will deepen their own understanding. Step three is adoption. So, this is people actually using the AI tools in their daily work. This is beyond procuring the technology and understanding how to use the technology, but it's actually changing people's behaviors and their habits to integrate AI into their daily work. There are three key things here for getting started. The first is doing a time study. So, having individual team members look at their calendars and identify, "What am I spending the most time on? Or what is draining my energy the most?" Because the answer to that question is going to identify the highest points of leverage for them. The second is to start small. Instead of trying to automate the entire sales pipeline in one shot, start with a specific piece of it that's the most time-consuming or the most draining. Finally, people need to shift their thinking from these generic use cases to very hyper-specific ones as if they are the only person in the world that will benefit from this automation. The journey for this will typically look like this. People will start with these bolted on AI features like summarize this email or draft a reply or all these little sparkles that you'll see on your existing software applications. They'll start just by using those AI features. Then they'll start using these proper AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini to give them ad hoc requests. So, instead of just clicking summarize this email and getting a generic response, maybe they want to summarize the entire transcript of a meeting and they want it to be formatted in a very specific way, or they're trying to ask questions of that transcript to get a very specific piece of information. Then, over time, as they start doing the same tasks over and over again in this ad hoc kind of way with these AI tools, it'll turn into task-specific prompts. So, if they find themselves generating some artifact after each meeting and they're using the same exact prompt every single time, they're going to have this library of prompts that is growing and evolving over time for specific tasks. And ultimately, the end state here is to have custom agents for specific tasks. So, instead of having to manually copy-paste a prompt into ChatGPT and pasting a transcript, you just connect the right tools to ChatGPT so that it can automatically fetch the transcript, generate the artifact, and then upload that artifact to OneDrive or Notion or to email it out to the team without the human having to copy and paste all this information across different software applications. So, if you're curious on what this might look like, in my previous video, I actually showed how I've been automating my non-coding work using Claude Code. There I talked through a few use cases of how I used MCP and agent skills to automate three non-coding tasks. Finally, we have the redesign step. This is the end state of the AI transformation, which is building AI-native business systems, not just these ad hoc automations. Few key transformations are going to happen at this phase. Over time, AI goes from this thing that's just bolted on to your business processes to being fully integrated in the process. Another thing that will naturally happen is that job descriptions will evolve and change. Just like with that sales outreach example, the GTM person's role went from working in the sales workflow to working on the workflow. So, the work that humans do is naturally going to evolve from being defined by the inputs to the outputs. So, instead of it being about the work that the humans need to do, it's all about the outcomes that they are responsible for. Ultimately, at this stage, mindsets will start to shift from executing work to owning outcomes. Because any process that happens over and over again in a somewhat predictable way can be delegated to an AI agent. I talked about a lot of different things in this video, so I just wanted to recap with a few key takeaways. The first is without a clear goal for AI, it's easy for teams to expend a lot of effort, but make little progress. Ultimately, if your goal is AI transformation, what that means is rebuilding your business systems with AI fully integrated. Here I shared a four-step roadmap for doing this, starting with equipping your team with the right tools, educating them on a shared understanding and best practices on how to use the tools, having people actually change their behavior and adopt the technology in their day-to-day work, and then finally redesigning business systems with these new found automations and AI agents. During this transformation, things will evolve. Tools will evolve. You'll pick up new tools, you'll drop obsolete ones, new business processes will be created, some steps will be added, some steps will be removed, and job descriptions will evolve. People will shift from focusing on inputs to focusing on the outputs of workflows. And ultimately, the The of humans will shift to higher leverage work. It'll shift from doing inputs to owning outcomes.
